Commercial Apr 5, 2026

5 Aircraft With The Most Powerful Jet Engines

The aircraft flying today are dominated by the Boeing 777 family of giant twin-jets, with Airbus' rival A350 coming in behind.

The GE90 and similar ultra-high-bypass engines powering widebody twins deliver 110,000+ lbf thrust, enabling operators to maximize payload-range economics on long-haul routes.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 5, 2026

Is It True That The Airbus A321XLR’s Fuel Tank Is Visible From The Outside?

The plane's massive fuel tanks create a noticeable difference in appearance compared to other A321neo variants.

The A321XLR's extended fuel tank geometry creates visible structural bulges that serve as a distinctive identifier and represent a design trade-off between range capability and conventional aesthetics.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 5, 2026

Inside The Airbus A380 Staircase Structure

Having two decks on an aircraft is no easy feat to manage. The Airbus A380 reimagined how stairs can be used onboard, doing so on a massive scale.

The A380's innovative staircase design enables efficient passenger flow between decks while optimizing the structural integrity and weight distribution critical to the world's largest passenger aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Safety Apr 5, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Effective safety risk management requires organizations to systematically document why certain residual risks are acceptable after implementing feasible controls, creating defensible compliance records.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Apr 5, 2026

Delta One "Basic": It's the Same Seat With Fewer Perks

As more and more airlines move unbundling further up the cabin, the carrier adds to this growing list.

Delta's tiered premium cabin strategy signals airlines are capturing incremental revenue from premium customers unwilling to pay full business-class fares.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 4, 2026

5 Aircraft That Still Play A Key Role In US Defense Strategy

The F-16 plays a leading role in the defense of the Lower 48, while a different range of aircraft are used to patrol Alaska.

The US maintains geographically differentiated air defense capabilities, with F-16s protecting the continental US while specialized aircraft handle Alaska's unique operational requirements.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

This Airline Sacrifices Revenue Seats To Give You The Most Spacious Economy Class In 2026

This carrier offers passengers exceptional legroom.

By permanently reducing seat density, this airline is betting that premium economy positioning and ancillary revenue will offset lost capacity per flight.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Apr 5, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

SMS Declaration of Compliance demonstrates operators' commitment to safety management system frameworks, with FAA audits focusing on actual implementation effectiveness rather than documentation alone.

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

How Much Does It Cost To Operate A Boeing 777 In 2026?

The Boeing 777 is the world's largest twinjet and is one of the workhorses of long-haul routes, although its operating expenses are high.

Rising fuel, maintenance, and labor costs project Boeing 777 hourly operating expenses to exceed $15,000 by 2026, pressuring airlines to optimize utilization on high-yield routes.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 4, 2026

U.S. F-16 Shown with Mine-Dispensing Cluster Bombs

CENTCOM has released photos of F-16s loaded with cluster bombs for operations in Iran, a week after BLU-91/B ‘Gator’ anti-tank mines were found on the ground. The U.S. Central Command has released on Apr. 4, 2026, new photos of U.S. Air Force F-16CM Fighting Falcon fighter jets (specifically Block...

CENTCOM's public release of F-16 cluster munition imagery signals escalating operational posture and willingness to visibly demonstrate anti-armor capabilities in the region.

See The Aviationist →

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

The 5 Longest US Domestic Flights You Can Fly Right Now

Domestic travel within the US can be quite the ordeal, with some routes nearing 5,000 miles.

Ultra-long domestic routes like Hawaii-East Coast flights now represent significant fuel and crew scheduling challenges that reshape regional hub economics.

See Simple Flying →

▶20:47
Regulatory Apr 5, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Policy & Safety Promotion

This video provides an overview of implementing a safety management system (SMS), specifically aligned with FAA Part 5 regulations. Join us as we explore Blue Skies Charter, a fictional company, and learn about general SMS requirements, safety policy development and safety promotion strategies. This video covers essential components like leadership commitment, gap analysis, safety objectives, emergency response planning and employee training. Whether you're a small charter operator or interested in aviation safety, this video offers valuable insights into building an effective SMS.

Small operators can systematically reduce risk through structured SMS implementation by prioritizing leadership commitment, gap analysis, and employee training rather than reactive safety measures.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Safety Apr 4, 2026

Baby Kennedy! Passenger Gives Birth Onboard Caribbean Airlines Flight At New York-JFK

This certainly was an unusual occurrence.

In-flight medical emergencies requiring immediate delivery demand airlines maintain advanced obstetric training protocols and rapid diversion capabilities to nearest suitable airports.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Atlas 5 launches Amazon Leo satellites

An Atlas 5 launched the latest set of satellites for Amazon’s broadband constellation April 4 as the company seeks to accelerate deployment of its spacecraft.

Amazon's accelerated satellite deployment signals intensifying competition for low-earth-orbit broadband dominance against Starlink and Kuiper.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

The Striking Differences Between The Boeing 747-8 & Airbus A380

Both quadjets are massive and serve several high-capacity routes.

The 747-8's newer technology and lower operating costs give it competitive advantages over the aging A380 despite similar cabin sizes, influencing airline retirement and fleet modernization decisions.

See Simple Flying →

▶15:03
Safety Apr 5, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Assurance

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety assurance, which evaluates the continued effectiveness of implemented risk control strategies and supports the identification of new hazards. Whether you're an owner, operator or part of a small aviation team, this guide provides valuable insights and tools to enhance your safety management practices and ensure a safer operational environment.

Small operators can leverage structured safety assurance processes to systematically validate risk controls and detect emerging hazards without requiring enterprise-level SMS infrastructure.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

NASA Answers Your Most Pressing Artemis II Questions

The first crewed test flight under NASA’s Artemis program is underway. Four Artemis II astronauts are flying aboard NASA’s Orion spacecraft around the Moon and back, as they test how the spacecraft’s systems operate in a deep space environment. NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and...

Artemis II validates deep-space vehicle performance in lunar orbit before humans return to the Moon's surface, reducing risk for subsequent crewed missions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

SES eyes cross-unit content bundles between media and aero

Multi-orbit satellite operator SES is exploring cross-unit content bundles between its media and aero or cruise businesses, as the passenger experience evolves. The revelation, made by SES senior product executive Blane Boynton during the SATShow conference and exhibition in Washington D.C., comes...

SES is monetizing converged services by bundling satellite-delivered media with in-flight connectivity, positioning integrated content distribution as a competitive advantage in evolving passenger experience markets.

See Runway Girl Network →

Military Apr 4, 2026

Mission to Recover Downed F-15E Aircrew Puts Combat Search And Rescue in the Spotlight

The loss of an F-15E Strike Eagle in Iran prompted specialized Combat Search and Rescue units to spring into action, with a multitude of assets working together to recover all missing aircrew. Here’s how. On Apr. 3, 2026, multiple specialized units sprang into action for a complex mission they...

Modern CSAR operations require unprecedented coordination across multiple specialized units and assets to execute successful aircrew recovery in hostile territory.

See The Aviationist →

Industry Apr 5, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization through technology integration offers airlines and airports a near-term path to handle projected traffic growth without proportional emissions increases.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Space Force budget would more than double in Trump’s $1.5 trillion defense plan

$71 billion request for the U.S. Space Force for fiscal year 2027 includes more than $60 billion for procurement, research and development...

Massive Space Force budget increase signals accelerated investment in space-based infrastructure that will reshape orbital operations and satellite-dependent aviation systems.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 4, 2026

Iran’s RQ-170 Clone Destroyed in U.S. Strikes, CENTCOM Video Shows

A video shared by CENTCOM shows the destruction of an Iranian clone of the RQ-170 Sentinel, which was reverse-engineered after crashing in Iran 15 years ago. An Iranian clone of the secretive U.S. RQ-170 Sentinel ISR drone was apparently destroyed in an airstrike, an Apr. 2, 2026 video by the U.S....

Iran's reverse-engineered RQ-170 drone demonstrates how advanced military UAV technology transfers create operational threats requiring active U.S. counter-measures in contested airspace.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Illuminated in Orion

NASA astronaut Christina Koch reads on a tablet in the dimly lit Orion crew capsule in this April 3, 2026, photo. To the right of the image’s center, CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen is seen in profile peering out of one of Orion’s windows. Lights are turned off to avoid glare on...

Orion's lighting design must balance astronaut visibility and operational needs while managing window glare during extended lunar missions.

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Artemis 2 in good shape cruising towards the moon

A day after lighting its engine to head to the moon, the Artemis 2 Orion spacecraft is performing well with only minor issues.

Artemis 2's successful trans-lunar injection with minimal anomalies validates NASA's deep-space propulsion systems for sustained crewed lunar missions.

Regulatory Apr 4, 2026

Trump budget seeks $372 million cut to US Essential Air Service program

US President Donald Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget proposal takes aim at the Essential Air Service program, calling for…...

Eliminating EAS subsidies could strand 43 rural communities from commercial air access, forcing consolidation onto hub-dependent networks and increasing operational costs for regional carriers.

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

NASA Sets Coverage for Northrop Grumman’s CRS-24 Resupply Launch

NASA, Northrop Grumman, and SpaceX are targeting no earlier than 8:49 a.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 8, for the next launch delivering science investigations, supplies, and equipment to the International Space Station. Filled with approximately 11,000 pounds of cargo, the Northrop Grumman Cygnus XL...

Northrop Grumman's larger Cygnus XL variant will deliver 11,000 pounds of cargo to the ISS, demonstrating expanded commercial resupply capacity for sustained space station operations.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US A-10 hit during Iran rescue mission, pilot ejects and is rescued

A US Air Force A-10 was hit during combat operations tied to the war with Iran on April…...

Combat damage to legacy platforms like the A-10 during sustained Middle East operations highlights the operational costs of extended regional conflicts on aging airframe availability.

Commercial Apr 3, 2026

Press Release: SUM Air orders up to eight ATR 72-600s

ATR, the world’s leading regional aircraft manufacturer, today announced that SUM Air, Korea’s newest regional airline, has placed an order for four brand‑new ATR 72‑600 aircraft, along with four additional purchase rights, with deliveries scheduled from 2028. The agreement was signed on the...

SUM Air's staggered 2028+ delivery timeline suggests ATR's production capacity remains constrained despite strong regional aircraft demand from emerging carriers.

See Runway Girl Network →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

Michael Graham Named NTSB Vice Chairman

The certified transport pilot and NTSB board member will co-head the agency for a three-year term.

Graham's piloting background positions the NTSB to strengthen accident investigation credibility with the aviation community during his three-year vice chairmanship.

See AVweb →

Safety Apr 3, 2026

US and Europe revise GPS interference guidelines as spoofing risks grow

Aviation authorities are warning that instances of GPS interference have spread across a widening range of high-risk airspace,…...

Growing GPS spoofing incidents are forcing regulators to establish stricter interference detection standards, requiring airlines to invest in backup navigation systems beyond traditional GPS reliance.

Military Apr 3, 2026

Updated: A-10 Thunderbolt II Crashed Near the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Officials Tell NYT

Almost the same time as an F-15E Strike Eagle went down over Iran, the U.S. Air Force lost an A-10 Thunderbolt II attack aircraft near the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. officials told NYT that the jet’s pilot was safely rescued. While search and rescue efforts for at least one of the crew members of an […]...

The near-simultaneous loss of two U.S. Air Force aircraft in the Middle East region signals intensified operational tempo or heightened threat levels requiring immediate force readiness assessment.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Meet NASA’s New Artemis II Science Officers

NASA’s first Artemis II science officers—Kelsey Young, Trevor Graff, and Angela Garcia—are pioneering a new era of mission operations by integrating advanced lunar science directly into Mission Control. Their work will shape how future Artemis crews observe, analyze, and deepen our understanding of...

New Science Officer roles embedded in Mission Control represent a structural shift in how lunar exploration will be conducted, enabling real-time scientific decision-making during Artemis missions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Italy’s Argotec plans to scale Florida satellite facility to meet rising US demand

Italy’s Argotec has officially opened its first U.S. satellite production facility, cementing a foothold near Kennedy Space Center in Florida to join other foreign space firms pursuing growing demand from American programs.

Foreign satellite manufacturers are strategically locating U.S. production facilities near launch hubs to capture defense and commercial space contracts, signaling consolidation of aerospace supply chains.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US F-15E Shot Down Over Iran, One Crew Member Rescued

The fate of the aircraft’s second crew member is unknown.

Loss of advanced fighter aircraft over hostile territory raises critical questions about air defense vulnerabilities and search-and-rescue operational constraints in contested airspace.

FAA report proposes sweeping rewrite of Part 141 flight training rules

A 471-page industry report released through the FAA’s Part 141 modernization docket proposes a sweeping rewrite of how…...

The comprehensive modernization proposal signals the FAA's recognition that current Part 141 standards no longer match contemporary aircraft technology, training methods, and workforce demands.
